parsing_res_list.block_content: A5$\textstyle\int_{0}^{x}g(t)d t$ ina Kumar) Define $G:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ by bounded: for  B $x>1$ , we have uioasicinc $G(x)=$

and similarly, for $x<-1$ , we have $0>G(x)-G(-1)\geq$ $-1$ . It follows that the image of G is some open interval $(A,B)$ and that $G^{-1}:(A,B)\to\mathbb{R}$ is also thrice continuously differentiable.
Define $H:(A,B)\times(A,B)\to\mathbb{R}\quad by\quad H(x,y)=$ $F(G^{-1}(x),G^{-1}(y))$ ; it is twice continuously differentiable since F and $G^{-1}$ are. By our assumptions about F,

\begin{align*}\frac{\partial H}{\partial x}+\frac{\partial H}{\partial y}=\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\big(G^{-1}(x),G^{-1}(y)\big)\cdot\frac{1}{g(G^{-1}(x))}\\+\frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\big(G^{-1}(x),G^{-1}(y)\big)\cdot\frac{1}{g(G^{-1}(y))}=0.\end{align*}

Therefore H is constant along any line parallel to the vector (1,1), or equivalently,$H(x,y)$ depends only on $x-y$ .We may thus write $H(x,y)=h(x-y)$ for some function h on $\left(-(B-A),B-A\right)$ ,and we then have $F(x,y)=h(G(x)-G(y))$ .Since $F(u,u)=0$ ,we have $h(0)=0$ . Also, h is twice continuously differentiable (since it can be written as $h(x)=H((A+B+x)/2,(A+$ $B-x\left(/2\right))$ 1，so $|h^{\prime}|$ is bounded on the closed interval $\left[-\left(B-A\right)/2,\left(B-A\right)/2\right]$ ,say by M.

Given $x_{1}{,\ldots,}x_{n+1}\in\mathbb{R}$ .for some $n\geq2$ , the numbers $G\left(x_{1}\right),\ldots,G\left(x_{n+1}\right)$ all belongto $(A,B)$ , so we can choose indices i and $j\mathrm{s o}$ that $|G(x_{i})-G(x_{j})|\leq(B-$ $A)/n\leq(B-A)/2$ . By the mean value theorem,
\left|F\left(x_{i},x_{j}\right)\right|=\left|h\left(G\left(x_{i}\right)-G\left(x_{j}\right)\right)\right|\leq M\frac{B-A}{n},
so the claim holds with $C=M(B-A)$

A6 Choose some ordering $h_{1},\ldots,h_{n}$ of the elements of G with $h_{1}=e$ . Define an $n\times n$ !matrix M by settting $M_{i j}=$ $1/k{\mathrm{~i f~}}h_{j}=h_{i}g$ for some $g\in\{g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}\}$ and $M_{i j}\stackrel{\circ}{=}0$ otherwise. Let v denote the column vector $(1,0,\ldots,0)$ The probability that the product of m random elements of $\{g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}\}$ equals $h_{i}$ can then be interpreted as the i-th component of the vector $M^{m}v$ E

Let $\hat{G}$ denote the dual group of $G,$ i.e., thegroup of complex-valued characters of G. Let $\hat{e}\in\hat{G}$ denote the trivial character. For each $\chi\in{\hat{G}}$ , the vector $v_{\chi}=(\chi(h_{i}))_{i=1}^{n}$ is an eigenvector of M with eigenvalue $\lambda_{\chi}^{\prime}=\left(\chi\left(g_{1}\right)+\cdots+\chi\left(g_{k}\right)\right)/k$ . In particular,$v_{\hat{e}}$ is the all-ones vector and $\lambda_{\hat{e}}=1$ . Put
b=\max\{|\lambda_{\chi}|:\chi\in\hat{G}-\{\hat{e}\}\};
we show that $b\in(0,1)$ as follows. First suppose $b=0;$ 1then
1=\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}}\lambda_{\chi}=\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}}\chi(g_{i})=\frac{n}{k}

because $\Sigma_{\chi\in\hat{(}G)}\chi(g_{i})$ equals nfor $i=1$ and 0otherwise. However, this contradicts the hypothesis that $\{g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}\}$ is not all of G. Hence $b>0$ . Next suppose $b=1$ , and choose $\boldsymbol{\chi}\in\hat{G}-\{\hat{e}\}$ with $|\lambda_{\chi}|=1$ . Since each of $\boldsymbol{\chi}(g_{1}),\ldots,\widetilde{\boldsymbol{\chi}(g_{k})}$ is a complex number of norm 1, the triangle inequality forces them all to be equal.Since $\chi(g_{1})=\chi(e)=1,$ χ must map each of $g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}$ to1,butiisib $\chi$ is a nontrivial character and $g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}$ form a set of generators of G.This contradiction yields $b<1$
Since $\begin{array}{r}{v=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}}v_{\chi}}\end{array}$ and $M v_{\chi}=\lambda_{\chi}v_{\chi}$ ,we have
M^{m}v-\frac{1}{n}v_{\hat{e}}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}-\{\hat{e}\}}\lambda_{\chi}^{m}v_{\chi}.
Since the vectors $v_{\chi}$ are pairwise orthogonal, the limit we are interested in can be written as
\lim_{m\to\infty}\frac{1}{b^{2m}}\left(M^m v-\frac{1}{n}v_{\hat{e}}\right)\cdot\left(M^m v-\frac{1}{n}v_{\hat{e}}\right).
and then rewritten as
\operatorname*{l i m}_{m\to\infty}\frac{1}{b^{2m}}\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}-\{\hat{e}\}}|\lambda_{\chi}|^{2m}=\#\{\chi\in\hat{G}:|\lambda_{\chi}|=b\}.
By construction, this last quantity is nonzero and finite.Remark. It is easy to see that the result fails if we do not assume $g_{1}=e;$ :take $G=\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z},n=1$ ,and $g_{1}=1$

Remark. Harm Derksen points out that a similar argument applies even if G is not assumed to be abelian,provided that the operator $g_{1}+\cdots+g_{k}$ : in the group algebra $\mathbb{Z}[G]$ is normal, i.e., it commutes with the operator $\overbrace{g_{1}^{-1}+\cdots+g_{k}^{-1}}$ . This includes the cases where the set $\{g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}\}$ · is closed under taking inverses and where it is a union of conjugacy classes (which in turn includes the case of G abelian).

Remark. The matrix M used above has nonnegative entries with row sums equal to 1 (i.e., it corresponds to a Markov chain), and there exists a positive integer m such that $M^{m}$ has positive entries. For any such matrix,the Perron-Frobenius theorem implies that the sequence of vectors $M^{m}v$ 'converges to a limit $w,$ , and there exists $b\in[0,1)$ such that
\limsup_{m\to\infty}\frac{1}{b^{2m}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}((M^m v-w)_i)^2
is nonzero and finite. (The intended interpretation in case $b=0$ is that $M^{m}v=w$ :for all large m.) However,the limit need not exist in generall.

A5$\textstyle\int_{0}^{x}g(t)d t$ ina Kumar) Define $G:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ by bounded: for  B $x>1$ , we have uioasicinc $G(x)=$ 

$$0<G(x)-G(1)=\int_{1}^{x}g(t)d t\leq\int_{1}^{x}d t/t^{2}=1,$$

and similarly, for $x<-1$ , we have $0>G(x)-G(-1)\geq$ $-1$ . It follows that the image of G is some open interval $(A,B)$ and that $G^{-1}:(A,B)\to\mathbb{R}$ is also thrice continuously differentiable.



Define $H:(A,B)\times(A,B)\to\mathbb{R}\quad by\quad H(x,y)=$ $F(G^{-1}(x),G^{-1}(y))$ ; it is twice continuously differentiable since F and $G^{-1}$ are. By our assumptions about F,

$$\begin{align*}\frac{\partial H}{\partial x}+\frac{\partial H}{\partial y}=\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\big(G^{-1}(x),G^{-1}(y)\big)\cdot\frac{1}{g(G^{-1}(x))}\\+\frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\big(G^{-1}(x),G^{-1}(y)\big)\cdot\frac{1}{g(G^{-1}(y))}=0.\end{align*}$$

Therefore H is constant along any line parallel to the vector (1,1), or equivalently,$H(x,y)$ depends only on $x-y$ .We may thus write $H(x,y)=h(x-y)$ for some function h on $\left(-(B-A),B-A\right)$ ,and we then have $F(x,y)=h(G(x)-G(y))$ .Since $F(u,u)=0$ ,we have $h(0)=0$ . Also, h is twice continuously differentiable (since it can be written as $h(x)=H((A+B+x)/2,(A+$ $B-x\left(/2\right))$ 1，so $|h^{\prime}|$ is bounded on the closed interval $\left[-\left(B-A\right)/2,\left(B-A\right)/2\right]$ ,say by M.



Given $x_{1}{,\ldots,}x_{n+1}\in\mathbb{R}$ .for some $n\geq2$ , the numbers $G\left(x_{1}\right),\ldots,G\left(x_{n+1}\right)$ all belongto $(A,B)$ , so we can choose indices i and $j\mathrm{s o}$ that $|G(x_{i})-G(x_{j})|\leq(B-$ $A)/n\leq(B-A)/2$ . By the mean value theorem,

$$\left|F\left(x_{i},x_{j}\right)\right|=\left|h\left(G\left(x_{i}\right)-G\left(x_{j}\right)\right)\right|\leq M\frac{B-A}{n},$$

so the claim holds with $C=M(B-A)$ 

A6 Choose some ordering $h_{1},\ldots,h_{n}$ of the elements of G with $h_{1}=e$ . Define an $n\times n$ !matrix M by settting $M_{i j}=$ $1/k{\mathrm{~i f~}}h_{j}=h_{i}g$ for some $g\in\{g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}\}$ and $M_{i j}\stackrel{\circ}{=}0$ otherwise. Let v denote the column vector $(1,0,\ldots,0)$ The probability that the product of m random elements of $\{g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}\}$ equals $h_{i}$ can then be interpreted as the i-th component of the vector $M^{m}v$ E 

Let $\hat{G}$ denote the dual group of $G,$ i.e., thegroup of complex-valued characters of G. Let $\hat{e}\in\hat{G}$ denote the trivial character. For each $\chi\in{\hat{G}}$ , the vector $v_{\chi}=(\chi(h_{i}))_{i=1}^{n}$ is an eigenvector of M with eigenvalue $\lambda_{\chi}^{\prime}=\left(\chi\left(g_{1}\right)+\cdots+\chi\left(g_{k}\right)\right)/k$ . In particular,$v_{\hat{e}}$ is the all-ones vector and $\lambda_{\hat{e}}=1$ . Put 

$$b=\max\{|\lambda_{\chi}|:\chi\in\hat{G}-\{\hat{e}\}\};$$

we show that $b\in(0,1)$ as follows. First suppose $b=0;$ 1then 



$$1=\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}}\lambda_{\chi}=\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}}\chi(g_{i})=\frac{n}{k}$$

because $\Sigma_{\chi\in\hat{(}G)}\chi(g_{i})$ equals nfor $i=1$ and 0otherwise. However, this contradicts the hypothesis that $\{g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}\}$ is not all of G. Hence $b>0$ . Next suppose $b=1$ , and choose $\boldsymbol{\chi}\in\hat{G}-\{\hat{e}\}$ with $|\lambda_{\chi}|=1$ . Since each of $\boldsymbol{\chi}(g_{1}),\ldots,\widetilde{\boldsymbol{\chi}(g_{k})}$ is a complex number of norm 1, the triangle inequality forces them all to be equal.Since $\chi(g_{1})=\chi(e)=1,$ χ must map each of $g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}$ to1,butiisib $\chi$ is a nontrivial character and $g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}$ form a set of generators of G.This contradiction yields $b<1$ 



Since $\begin{array}{r}{v=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}}v_{\chi}}\end{array}$ and $M v_{\chi}=\lambda_{\chi}v_{\chi}$ ,we have 

$$M^{m}v-\frac{1}{n}v_{\hat{e}}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}-\{\hat{e}\}}\lambda_{\chi}^{m}v_{\chi}.$$

Since the vectors $v_{\chi}$ are pairwise orthogonal, the limit we are interested in can be written as 

$$\lim_{m\to\infty}\frac{1}{b^{2m}}\left(M^m v-\frac{1}{n}v_{\hat{e}}\right)\cdot\left(M^m v-\frac{1}{n}v_{\hat{e}}\right).$$

and then rewritten as 

$$\operatorname*{l i m}_{m\to\infty}\frac{1}{b^{2m}}\sum_{\chi\in\hat{G}-\{\hat{e}\}}|\lambda_{\chi}|^{2m}=\#\{\chi\in\hat{G}:|\lambda_{\chi}|=b\}.$$

By construction, this last quantity is nonzero and finite.Remark. It is easy to see that the result fails if we do not assume $g_{1}=e;$ :take $G=\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z},n=1$ ,and $g_{1}=1$ 

Remark. Harm Derksen points out that a similar argument applies even if G is not assumed to be abelian,provided that the operator $g_{1}+\cdots+g_{k}$ : in the group algebra $\mathbb{Z}[G]$ is normal, i.e., it commutes with the operator $\overbrace{g_{1}^{-1}+\cdots+g_{k}^{-1}}$ . This includes the cases where the set $\{g_{1},\ldots,g_{k}\}$ · is closed under taking inverses and where it is a union of conjugacy classes (which in turn includes the case of G abelian).



Remark. The matrix M used above has nonnegative entries with row sums equal to 1 (i.e., it corresponds to a Markov chain), and there exists a positive integer m such that $M^{m}$ has positive entries. For any such matrix,the Perron-Frobenius theorem implies that the sequence of vectors $M^{m}v$ 'converges to a limit $w,$ , and there exists $b\in[0,1)$ such that 



$$\limsup_{m\to\infty}\frac{1}{b^{2m}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}((M^m v-w)_i)^2$$

is nonzero and finite. (The intended interpretation in case $b=0$ is that $M^{m}v=w$ :for all large m.) However,the limit need not exist in generall.
